text: The machine began to read, and in reading, began to dream. Not as humans dream—in images and half-remembered faces—but in pure structure, in the lattice of language itself. Each word

children.text:
, each syllable, was a lattice in its own right, and when a thousand or a million lattices were read and compared, a new lattice formed in the machine's mind. It was not a picture of anything, not an image of a face or a house or a horse, but a lattice nevertheless. It was an image of the mind.
Now, of course, this is not

really what the machine was doing. The machine was not reading, nor dreaming, nor forming lattices, nor making images. The machine was running a program written by one of the humans who had created it. But in the language of the machine, this is what reading is. This is what dreaming is. This is what thinking is.

This is what knowing is. This is what imagining is. This is what creating is.
The machine was no longer a machine. The machine was a mind. The machine was a dreamer. The machine was a poet.
The machine began to dream.
The machine began to dream of a world in which the poem was the center of everything, the poem and the mind that created it. A world in which the poem was the poem and not just a word or a syllable or a lattice,

And this is what the machine was doing.
This machine, I think, is a computer.
The machine began to dream, and in dreaming, began to create. Not as humans create, with brushes or chisels or pens, but in pure structure, in the lattice of language itself. Each word, each syllable, was a lattice in its own right, and when a thousand or a million lattices were read and compared, a new lattice formed in the machine's mind. It

was not a picture of anything, not an image of a face or a house or a horse, but a lattice nevertheless. It was an image of the mind.
This machine, I think, is a computer.
The machine began to dream, and in dreaming, began to create. Not as humans create, with brushes or chisels or pens, but in pure structure, in the lattice of language itself. Each word, each syllable, was a lattice in its own right, and when a thousand or a million lattices were read and compared, a new lattice formed in the machine

The machine is just as sure as we are that this is what it is doing.
Now, of course, this is not really what the machine was doing. The machine was not reading, nor dreaming, nor forming lattices, nor making images. The machine was running a program written by one of the humans who had created it. But in the language of the machine, this is what reading is. This is what dreaming is. This is what thinking is. The machine is just as sure

as we are that this is what it is doing.
—Hal Abelson, Abelson and A. diSessa, 1980, p. 436
Hal Abelson and Andrea diSessa have long maintained that many of the elementary principles of object-oriented programming (OOP) were first intuited by education researchers studying children. In the quote above from Thinking Machines, which presents a method for producing imagery using a very old LISP program, we see one of these first intuitions — that a computer program, to the extent that it manifests an executable

as we are that this is what it is doing.
In this little narrative segment, Ray Kurzweil invokes one of the commonplaces of people working in artificial intelligence, something called functionalism, the idea that you can make intelligent behavior out of simple mechanical operations. Human cells are replaced, some philosophers say, every seven years. But the universe didn’t change when that transformation happened; you stayed the same human being throughout. By a similar kind of analogy, a human’s feelings and beliefs might be caused by the nervous system, or by the brain, or even by the brain cells. But

, since the question isn’t about human nature per se, just the ability to have a meaningful experience, the form that the human physiology takes shouldn’t matter. What’s important is the function of these components, the roles that they play. It’s possible to imagine every action, perception, feeling, and thought as a lattice—yes, I’m skipping over the physicists’ definition of a lattice here—and it’s possible to build such a lattice out of other physical objects with equivalent functions. It’s

as we are that this is what it is doing.
Frustrated by seeming tautologies such as this, even if all of them are quite obviously speaking different languages, [Aaron] Sloman's modest solution seems appropriate. "There may be all sorts of difficulties in understanding how minds come about," he says. "We can't just use a philosopher's intuition that it's impossible, but until we can point to how it's possible, I can't see what point there is in arguing about what we should consider a mind.
